name: SLB Limited

profile.description: SLB Limited (formerly Schlumberger) is the world's largest oilfield services company, providing technology, integrated project management, and information solutions to the oil and gas industry worldwide. The company operates through four divisions: Digital & Integration, Reservoir Performance, Well Construction, and Production Systems. Following the 2024 acquisition of ChampionX, SLB expanded its production chemicals and artificial lift capabilities. Under CEO Olivier Le Peuch since 2019, SLB has rebranded, embraced digital transformation, and committed to net-zero emissions by 2050 including Scope 3. Founded in 1926, headquartered in Houston, Texas.

innovation.label: DEVELOPING
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): SLB has executed meaningful transformation over the past five years — rebranding from Schlumberger to SLB to signal a broader energy technology identity, completing the $8B ChampionX acquisition in 2024 to build a production chemicals franchise, and launching the Lumi data and AI platform for next-generation oilfield digitalization [1][3]. Revenue grew from $28.1B in 2022 to $35.7B in 2025 (though partially acquisition-driven) and the company was the first in its sector to commit to Scope 3 net-zero. AI Deployment (1/2): The Lumi AI platform leverages machine learning for drilling optimization, reservoir modeling, and production analytics, providing quantifiable efficiency gains. SLB has embedded AI across its Digital & Integration division, which generates differentiated margins [3]. However, specific AI-related revenue figures are not separately disclosed. Market Share (1/2): SLB is the #1 global oilfield services company by revenue, with particular strength in international markets and deepwater operations. However, international revenue declined 4% in Q1 2026, and the company faces competitive pressure from Halliburton in North American completions [1]. Product Pipeline (1/2): Investments in the Lumi platform, carbon capture and storage services, geothermal energy, and production chemicals post-ChampionX expand the addressable market. R&D Intensity (1/1): R&D spending is above oilfield services industry average, supported by SLB's historically deep technology bench and patent portfolio [3].

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (3/4): SLB's purpose — 'driving energy innovation for a balanced planet' — is specific, thoughtful, and differentiates from peers by explicitly connecting the company's work to planetary sustainability rather than just serving the oil industry [3]. The rebrand from Schlumberger to SLB in 2022 was designed to reflect this broader energy identity. The purpose has guided strategic decisions including the first-in-sector Scope 3 net-zero commitment in 2020 and investments in carbon capture, geothermal, and hydrogen technologies. This is genuine purposefulness, though it hasn't yet been validated through a multi-decade, multi-leader test. Community Impact (1/2): SLB operates community development programs in operating regions and has committed to STEM education and workforce diversity initiatives, though the programs are not particularly distinctive for a company of this scale [4]. Employee Retention (1/2): CEO Le Peuch's approval rating is in the top 15% of similar-sized company CEOs on Comparably, and the executive team ranks in the top 30% [3]. However, Glassdoor reviews note significant senior and mid-level talent departures to big tech companies, creating leadership gaps. Insider Buying (1/2): Le Peuch's compensation is equity-heavy; no notable net open-market insider buying was identified in the last 12 months.

infrastructure.label: STRONG
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored as a dividend stock. D/E of 42.5% is well below the 70% target (score 1.0), reflecting conservative leverage [1]. ROE of 14.1% is just below the 15% target (score 0.94). Revenue growth YoY of 2.7% is below the 10% target (score 0.76), reflecting flat international activity and Middle East disruptions in Q1 2026 [1]. Revenue 5yr CAGR of ~8.3% is close to the 10% target (score 0.89). Forward P/E of 17.0x is below the 20x target (score 1.0). Profit margin of 9.3% is well below the 20% target (score 0.46), characteristic of the capital-intensive services model. Dividend metrics: payout ratio of 50.7% is above the 40% target (score 0.82); consecutive years paid of ~6 (restarted after 2020 cut) is below the 10 target (score 0.60, 2x weight); consecutive years growth of ~5 (score 0.50); 5yr dividend growth CAGR of 18.7% is exceptional (score 1.0, 2x weight), reflecting rapid dividend rebuilding from the 2020 cut. Q1 2026 revenue of $8.72B (+3% YoY) with net income of $752M [1].

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (7/9): SLB's core business — providing physical equipment, chemicals, crews, and engineering services to drill and complete oil and gas wells — is structurally protected from AI disruption. Oilfield operations require heavy machinery, trained field engineers, specialized chemicals, and physical presence at wellsites that AI cannot substitute [1]. The deeply regulated safety environment, harsh operating conditions (deepwater, high-pressure/high-temperature), and complex logistics create barriers beyond AI's reach. Moreover, AI is a clear demand driver — as AI data centers proliferate, electricity demand increases, supporting demand for the hydrocarbons SLB helps extract. However, AI-driven automation could reduce per-well crew sizes over time. AI Opportunity (1/1): The Lumi data and AI platform generates meaningful revenue through SLB's Digital & Integration division, providing AI-powered well planning, drilling optimization, and production analytics to E&P operators [3]. The ChampionX acquisition adds chemical optimization capabilities that benefit from machine learning. SLB's digital division carries higher margins than traditional services, and AI-enhanced offerings command premium pricing.

infinite_game.summary: SLB under Olivier Le Peuch has demonstrated genuine strategic vision — rebranding the company, committing to Scope 3 net-zero before any peer, acquiring ChampionX to build a production chemicals franchise, and launching the Lumi AI platform. The company's 100-year history of technology leadership and willingness to evolve its identity suggest infinite game thinking. However, SLB operates in a fundamentally cyclical industry where competitive dynamics are often zero-sum, and the purpose statement, while improved, is relatively new and untested through crisis.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): SLB's purpose — 'driving energy innovation for a balanced planet' — is genuine and distinctive for the oilfield services sector. The 2022 rebrand from Schlumberger to SLB was a strategic commitment to a broader energy identity beyond oil and gas [3]. The first-in-sector Scope 3 net-zero commitment (2020) and investments in carbon capture, geothermal, and hydrogen demonstrate the purpose guiding real capital allocation decisions. However, the purpose is relatively new (post-2019 under Le Peuch) and hasn't been tested through a major industry downturn or leadership transition. SLB's 100-year history was built on a different identity.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): Le Peuch's CEO approval rating in the top 15% of similar-sized companies and the executive team ranking in the top 30% suggest generally positive leadership [3]. SLB invests significantly in employee training and development. However, Glassdoor reviews note that many experienced senior and mid-level leaders left for big tech companies, creating leadership gaps. The oilfield services industry's cyclical nature — SLB cut ~20% of its workforce during the 2020 downturn — inherently undermines long-term trust. The 109,000-person global organization is adequate but not exceptional.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1): SLB's competitive approach under Le Peuch emphasizes technology differentiation and client value creation rather than rival destruction. The company's strategic response to Halliburton's North American completions strength has been to double down on international and digital capabilities rather than engage in a price war [1]. The ChampionX acquisition was framed as building unique capability, not defeating competitors. Management commentary focuses on expanding the addressable market and improving SLB's own offerings.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): SLB has evolved meaningfully — rebranding from Schlumberger, acquiring ChampionX ($8B), investing in carbon capture and geothermal, and building the Lumi AI platform represent genuine strategic evolution [1][3]. The company is actively positioning for a broader energy future beyond traditional oil and gas services. However, the core business remains oilfield services (95%+ of revenue), and the new energy ventures are early-stage. SLB has adapted and extended but not yet fundamentally reinvented its business model.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): Le Peuch demonstrated courage by committing to Scope 3 net-zero emissions in 2020 — a bold and potentially costly stance for an oilfield services company that drew both praise and criticism [4]. The $8B ChampionX acquisition represented a significant bet on production chemicals. The rebrand from the iconic Schlumberger name to SLB was a psychologically bold move. However, SLB's market-leading position and strong free cash flow (~$4B+ annual shareholder returns) mean these decisions carried less existential risk than they might for a smaller company.

investment_thesis: SLB delivered Q1 2026 revenue of $8.72B (+3% YoY) with the Production Systems division growing 23% to $3.51B driven by the ChampionX acquisition, while North America revenue surged 26% to $2.17B [1]. As the world's largest oilfield services company, SLB is uniquely positioned to benefit from the structural increase in energy demand driven by AI data centers, electrification, and emerging market industrialization. At 17.0x forward P/E with a 2.1% dividend yield (3.5% increase announced for 2026) and $4B+ in planned shareholder returns, SLB offers attractive income characteristics. The Lumi AI platform and ChampionX integration provide technology differentiation, and D/E of 42.5% reflects conservative leverage. The 18.7% 5-year dividend growth CAGR demonstrates rapid dividend rebuilding post-2020 cut.
thesis_continued: The primary risk is that global oilfield activity has plateaued. Total company revenue was essentially flat at $35.7B in 2025 vs. $36.3B in 2024, and international revenue declined 4% in Q1 2026 due to Middle East disruptions and customer budget discipline [1]. Net income fell 6% YoY in Q1 to $752M, and EPS declined 14% to $0.50, suggesting margin pressure even as revenue grew modestly. The 9.3% profit margin is structurally low for this capital-intensive model, limiting earnings leverage. The PEG ratio of 1.96 suggests the market is pricing in modest growth expectations. OPEC+ production decisions, oil price volatility, and the long-term energy transition create fundamental uncertainty about demand for traditional oilfield services. The 2020 dividend cut — though now well-recovered — demonstrates the inherent cyclical risk of this business model, and consecutive years paid of only 6 since the restart weighs on income quality metrics.
key_catalysts: ChampionX integration driving revenue synergies and margin expansion in the Production Systems division — Q1 2026 already showed 23% YoY growth in this segment
AI data center proliferation driving structural electricity demand increase, supporting long-term oil and gas investment and SLB's addressable market
Shareholder returns exceeding $4B in 2026 through dividends and $2.4B+ buybacks, representing ~5% of market cap and providing downside protection
key_risks: International revenue declined 4% in Q1 2026 due to Middle East disruptions and customer budget discipline — any sustained international downturn would disproportionately impact SLB given its ~75% international revenue mix
Net income declined 6% and EPS declined 14% in Q1 2026 despite revenue growth — margin compression from ChampionX integration costs and competitive pricing could persist
OPEC+ production decisions and oil price volatility create fundamental demand uncertainty — a prolonged low-price environment could trigger another activity downturn and potential dividend risk
